void.text: In 1981, Feynman envisioned a quantum computer. Shor and Grover turned it into revolutionary algorithms, and for decades laboratories fought to build stable qubits. By {{year}}, quantum computing was a consolidated but closed field: advances in papers, prototypes behind closed doors, collaboration measured in citations, not commits. On GitHub, absolute silence.

prelude.text: Then the industry decided to open its doors to the world…

genesis.title: Open-Source Genesis
genesis.milestoneIBM: the launch of IBM Quantum Experience (2016)
genesis.milestoneQiskit: the release of Qiskit and Q# (2017)
genesis.milestoneRigetti: the opening of Rigetti's Forest SDK
genesis.industryLeaders: Giants like {{names}} will lead the opening.
genesis.text: Year {{year}}: the era of open-source quantum begins.{{histContext}} {{total}} repos, {{totalOrgs}} organizations and {{totalUsers}} developers will materialize in this universe.{{industryContext}} What lived confined in laboratories is about to be built collaboratively.

firstNodes.title: The First Nodes
firstNodes.textIndustry: In {{year}}, {{orgCount}} organizations publish {{repoCount}} repos. {{orgNames}} open their code with projects like {{repoNames}}, laying the foundations of the open ecosystem.
firstNodes.textExperimental: The first {{repoCount}} repos of {{year}} don't come from large corporations: experimental projects like {{repoNames}}. Organizations like {{orgNames}} explore the terrain, paving the way for the major players.
firstNodes.textNoOrgs: In {{year}}, {{repoCount}} experimental repos emerge: {{repoNames}}. Small seeds planted before the industry recognized the potential of open-source quantum.

firstGiant.text: In {{year}}, {{label}} bursts in with {{repoCount}} repos and {{stars}} ★.{{flagship}}{{stack}}{{era}}
firstGiant.flagship: Its flagship project, {{name}} ({{stars}} ★), becomes a global reference.
firstGiant.sameEra: In this same period, {{names}} join in: the industry bets on open-source quantum.
firstGiant.sameEraDefault: The industry bets on building in the open.

acceleration.title: 2019 Acceleration
acceleration.text: 2019 marks a turning point in the ecosystem.{{growthContext}}{{orgsContext}} The race for quantum supremacy and growing global investment drive open-source activity to unprecedented levels.
acceleration.growth: {{count}} new repos raise the total to {{total}}.

competition.title: Structured Competition
competition.text: Between 2020 and 2021, the ecosystem enters structured competition: platforms, standards and communities.{{reposContext}}{{orgsContext}}{{collabsContext}} The ecosystem moves from isolated players to integrated industrial structure.
competition.collabs: {{count}} new inter-org collaboration links evidence increasingly dense networks.

epicenter.text: {{name}} occupies the gravitational center of the ecosystem: the most connected organization. {{repoCount}} repos and {{stars}} ★ accumulated.{{flagship}}{{stack}} A hub that attracts collaborators and generates influence in all directions.

stellarQubit.text: {{name}}: {{stars}} ★{{owner}}{{language}}. The most influential repo in the ecosystem. Other standouts: {{top3}}. In total, {{totalStars}} ★ accumulated across the entire ecosystem.

entanglement.title: Entanglement
entanglement.text: The ecosystem's strength lies in its connections. {{count}} real collaboration links unite {{orgCount}} organizations through developers who contribute to multiple projects. Strongest bonds: {{strongPairs}}. Each luminous arc is a bridge of talent between teams.

bridgeUsers.title: Bridge Users
bridgeUsers.text: Out of {{totalUsers}} developers, {{bridgeCount}} are bridge users ({{pct}}%): the golden dots. They contribute to repos from multiple organizations, weaving the collaboration network. Most active: {{topNames}}. Without them, the remaining {{normalCount}} would be fragmented into disconnected silos.

cosmicInflation.title: Cosmic Inflation
cosmicInflation.text: {{year}}: the ecosystem's biggest leap. {{growth}} new repos (+{{pct}}%).{{keyOrgsContext}}{{orgNames}} Cumulative: from {{prev}} to {{accum}} repos in twelve months.
cosmicInflation.keyOrgs1: {{names}} releases open-source code, catalyzing the expansion.
cosmicInflation.keyOrgsN: {{names}} release open-source code, catalyzing the expansion.

quantumBabel.title: Quantum Babel
quantumBabel.text: {{totalLangs}} languages coexist in the ecosystem. {{dominant}} dominates with {{pct}}%, but diversity is key: {{breakdown}}. From circuit simulation to hardware control, each language covers a specific need.

consolidation.title: Consolidation
consolidation.text: Since 2022: consolidation and maturity. {{repoCount}} repos ({{pct}}% of total){{orgsContext}}.{{concentrationContext}}{{collabsContext}} Specialized projects in tooling, middleware and error correction mark this era of maturity.
consolidation.decentral: The top 3 orgs concentrate only {{pct}}% of repos: knowledge is decentralizing.
consolidation.centralGrowing: The top 3 orgs concentrate {{pct}}%, but diversity grows.

finalPanorama.title: The Quantum Universe
finalPanorama.text: {{totalRepos}} repos, {{totalOrgs}} organizations, {{totalUsers}} developers, {{collabs}} real collaborations. Major hubs: {{topOrgs}}. From Feynman's intuition to a living, decentralized ecosystem where academia and industry build together the future of quantum computing.

entities.intro: Every visual element in the universe is an analogy with quantum physics. Software ecosystem entities (organizations, repositories, developers) are represented as interconnected quantum particles and systems.
entities.orgsDesc: The rotating rings represent GitHub organizations. Like a real quantum processor, each organization contains and manages repositories (qubits). Their concentric tori spin at different speeds, simulating the magnetic fields of a real processor. The central core glows with the organization's chromatic signature.
entities.reposDesc: The violet octahedra (diamond shapes) are repositories — the information unit of the ecosystem. The qubit is the unit of quantum information; analogously, each repository stores project knowledge. Their crystalline shape differentiates them from other entities. The probability clouds surrounding them represent their field of influence, and their size varies with repository activity.
entities.usersDesc: The green dots are developers who orbit the repositories they contribute to, like electrons orbiting an atom. Their orbital position is not fixed — they vibrate constantly (Heisenberg uncertainty principle). The orbital radius reflects their activity level in the repository.
entities.bridgeDesc: The golden dots flashing simultaneously are bridge users — developers who work across repositories from multiple organizations. Their synchronized flash visualizes quantum entanglement: instantaneous correlation regardless of distance. They are the most valuable entities in the ecosystem because they transfer knowledge between organizations.
entities.channelsDesc: The sinuous lavender waves between entities represent direct collaboration relationships (org→repo). Like quantum channels transmitting information between organizations and repositories, these wavy lines visualize the ecosystem's ownership structure. The color intensity and wave amplitude reflect the connection strength. Their lavender tone differentiates them from entities (cyan, violet, green).

quantum.intro: The universe is filled with quantum phenomena that bring life to the space between entities. Each visual effect has an analog in real quantum physics.
quantum.vacuumDesc: The space between entities is not empty: 200 particles of quantum foam flicker with creation/annihilation cycles (virtual particle-antiparticle pairs) and a Heisenberg jitter constantly displacing them. Below, an interference grid of 70×70 dots ripples with 5 superimposed wave sources, creating a tricolor pattern (green constructive, violet destructive, cyan nodes) that never repeats. Together they simulate quantum vacuum fluctuations.
quantum.genesisDesc: The initial white flash simulates the Big Bang that gives birth to the quantum universe. When entering the visualization, all entities are born from a singular point and expand following a kinetic animation that distributes organizations, repositories and users to their final positions. Opacity and scale increase progressively (entanglement phase).
quantum.cloudsDesc: The diffuse halos around each repository (qubit) are their probability clouds. In quantum mechanics, an electron's position is not a fixed point but a probability distribution. Cloud particles orbit at random speeds, pulsing with a faint glow reflecting the repository's superposition of states.
quantum.ringsDesc: The bright rings around organizations (processors) represent their energy levels. Like an atom with its electron shells, each organization emits rings reflecting its activity level. Colors inherit the organization's chromatic signature, and the rings pulse subtly with the ecosystem's rhythm.
quantum.tunnelDesc: The golden spheres traveling through entanglement channels are photons traversing potential barriers. In real physics, a particle can "tunnel" through a barrier that would classically be impenetrable. Here, photons travel between connected entities, visualizing active information transfer through the channels.
quantum.decoherenceDesc: The expanding rings periodically emanating from organizations simulate quantum decoherence — the process by which a quantum system loses its coherence through interaction with the environment. The waves expand and fade, representing how quantum information dissipates to the system's exterior.
quantum.hawkingDesc: The micro-particles constantly emanating from each organization simulate Hawking radiation — the thermal emission predicted by Stephen Hawking for black holes. Particles are born near the organization and slowly drift away, disappearing upon leaving its gravitational field. They represent the ecosystem's constant activity.
quantum.heisenbergDesc: The constant micro-vibration of all entities reflects the Heisenberg uncertainty principle: in the quantum world, absolutely fixed positions don't exist. You cannot simultaneously know a particle's position and momentum with infinite precision. Each node vibrates subtly as a reminder of this fundamental law.
quantum.interferenceDesc: A grid of 4,900 dots (70×70) on the horizontal plane forms the universe's "floor." 5 wave sources (one central high-frequency + 4 peripheral) generate an ever-changing interference pattern. Dots oscillate vertically based on combined amplitude and are colored in three tones: green (constructive maxima), violet (destructive) and cyan (nodes). Sources slowly shift, generating a texture that never repeats — like the double-slit experiment with multiple slits.
quantum.cosmicRaysDesc: Up to 8 simultaneous relativistic particles streaking through the universe with ribbon trails of 48 vertices and a palette of 6 colors (cyan, magenta, gold, emerald, plasma orange, violet). 55% are escape rays traveling at 1000–1500 u/s from the interior toward the Dyson Sphere — upon impact (R≥3500) they generate a shockwave and a flash matching the ray's color. The remaining 45% are slower normal rays crossing the interior without impact, like cosmic background radiation.
quantum.dysonDesc: A geodesic sphere (icosahedron subdivided 4×, ~1,280 triangles) at radius 3500 envelops the entire visible universe. Its edges emit a faint deep blue glow with 12 energy pulses continuously traveling along the mesh. When a cosmic ray hits, a Gaussian shockwave propagates, progressively illuminating the edges for 2.5s. When selecting an entity, impacts are attenuated 85% to avoid distraction.
quantum.gravWavesDesc: Up to 4 concentric rings emanate from the 8 most massive organizations in the ecosystem. Each wave expands across the horizontal plane for 4–7 seconds up to a radius of 90–150 units, in deep blue with additive blending. They only appear around organizations with visibility > 0.3 — as in general relativity, only the most massive bodies generate detectable gravitational perturbations.

analysis.intro: The quantum universe is not just an aesthetic visualization — each entity is analyzed with advanced network metrics that reveal the deep structure of collaboration.
analysis.radarDesc: The interactive pentagon in the detail panel shows 5 collaboration axes: Activity (commits, PRs), Diversity (distinct repos), Impact (ecosystem influence), Consistency (temporal regularity), and Reach (distinct organizations). Each axis is normalized from 0 to 1 and compared against the global average.
analysis.centralityDesc: Centrality measures an entity's structural importance within the graph. It uses algorithms like betweenness centrality (how many shortest paths pass through it) and degree centrality (number of connections). Entities with high centrality are critical for connectivity: if they disappear, the ecosystem fragments.
analysis.resilienceDesc: Resilience measures how many key contributors sustain a project. A value of 1 indicates a key pillar: an essential contributor keeping the project alive. With 2 it forms a reduced core, with 3-4 knowledge is balanced, and with 5+ there's high resilience. Shown with per-user contribution bars. Key pillars are valuable — diversifying contributors would increase the project's resilience.
analysis.communitiesDesc: The community detection algorithm identifies clusters of entities densely connected to each other but sparsely connected to the rest. Each community receives a unique color and entities are grouped visually. Zone borders (togglable from Settings) delimit these communities with translucent spheres.
analysis.rolesDesc: Each user receives a network role based on their connectivity metrics: Central Hub (many direct connections), Bridge (connects disjoint communities), Specialist (intense contributions in few repos), Explorer (dispersed contributions across many repos), or Collaborator (regular participation). The role is shown with a chromatic badge in the detail panel.
analysis.crossPollinationDesc: The cross-pollination rate measures what percentage of users contribute to repositories from multiple organizations, transferring practices and knowledge between teams. A high rate indicates a healthy ecosystem where ideas flow freely. Calculated as: bridge users / total users × 100.
analysis.dnaDesc: The Collaboration DNA is a unique visualization that encodes each entity's contribution pattern as a genetic chain. Each color segment represents an activity type (commits, issues, PRs, reviews), and the sequence reveals the developer's or team's work style — are they more of a reviewer, more of a coder, or well-balanced?
analysis.impactDesc: The impact simulation answers the question: "What would happen if this user stopped contributing?" It calculates which repositories would lose maintenance, how many connections would break, and how much the network would fragment. Classified as critical, high, or moderate impact.
analysis.similarDesc: The similarity engine finds entities with similar collaboration patterns using metrics like co-contribution (repos in common), language distribution, activity level, and connections. The similarity percentage indicates how interchangeable two entities are in the graph. Useful for finding backup candidates or like-minded collaborators.
analysis.healthDesc: The health gauge (semicircular arc) shows a composite score from 0-100 evaluating project sustainability. It combines metrics of: contributor diversity, recent activity, commit distribution, and resilience. The higher the score, the more resilient the project is against losing key contributors.
analysis.flowsDesc: Knowledge flows track how developers carry practices, tools, and patterns from one repository to another. A flow bar A → B indicates that developers working on A also contribute to B, creating a knowledge transfer channel. The bar width reflects the flow volume.

lenses.intro: Analytical lenses transform the universe's visualization to reveal different dimensions of the collaboration network. Each lens applies a coloring and sizing algorithm that highlights a specific aspect.
lenses.communitiesDesc: Colors each entity by the community it belongs to (Louvain/modularity algorithm). Same-colored entities form tightly-knit work clusters. Lets you identify organizational silos, isolated teams, and the bridge users connecting them. Zone borders are automatically enabled to delimit each community in 3D space.
lenses.communitiesNote: ✦ Multi-org Transition: users contributing to more than one organization smoothly transition between the community colors of each org they belong to. Transition rhythm varies randomly per user for an organic feel — watch how their particles shimmer between colors, visually revealing the human bridges between communities.
lenses.centralityDesc: Visualizes each entity's betweenness centrality — how many shortest paths in the network pass through it. More central entities appear larger and brighter, peripheral ones darken and shrink. Applied to all types: organizations, repositories, and users. Uses percentile rank (uniform distribution), so you'll always see a clear gradient from dark to bright regardless of data distribution.
lenses.resilienceDesc: Colors repositories by their key contributor distribution. Red = key pillar (1 main contributor), orange = reduced core (2), yellow = balanced (3-4), green = high resilience (5+). Organizations and users are dimmed so repos stand out. The most prominent repos have essential contributors sustaining them.
lenses.intensityDesc: Visualizes degree centrality — the number of direct connections each entity has. More connected entities appear larger with a warm golden glow, less connected ones darken. Uses percentile rank for uniform gradient distribution. Complements the Centrality lens: degree measures direct connections, betweenness measures importance as a bridge.
lenses.disciplinesDesc: Automatically classifies each user into one of 5 disciplines (Quantum Software, Quantum Physics, Quantum Hardware, Development Tooling, Education/Research) by analyzing their bios, organizations, programming languages, and repository topics. The cross-discipline index measures what percentage of collaborations cross disciplinary boundaries. Interdisciplinary bridges are users contributing to repos from ≥2 different disciplines.
lenses.disciplinesNote: ✦ Multidisciplinary Users: when a user has signals spread across 2 or more disciplines with no clear dominant one, they're classified as Multidisciplinary. With the lens active, these particles smoothly transition between their discipline colors — just like bridge users transition between community colors. Multidisciplinary profiles are the most valuable: they cross knowledge boundaries.
lenses.tunnelingDesc: The tunneling effect lets you find the shortest path between any two entities in the universe. Type the origin and destination in the search fields and the BFS algorithm will find the shortest connection chain. The result shows each hop between entities and its type (org → repo → user → repo → org). If there's no path, the entities are in disconnected graph components.

tour.intro: The Cosmic Tour is a cinematic guided experience that narrates the history of the open-source quantum computing ecosystem, from initial silence to its current expansion.
tour.startDesc: Press the Cosmic Tour button at the bottom to begin. The screen will fade to black and a narrative journey through the ecosystem's history will begin. During the tour, the camera moves automatically between waypoints while the temporal slider advances chronologically, revealing how the universe was formed.
tour.autoDesc: From the dashboard, the button that opens the Universe includes an auto-tour toggle. Activate it and enter the universe — the tour starts automatically with an immersive experience: a Big Bang effect gives way to Genesis without visible transitions, as if the universe were being born before your eyes.
tour.waypointsDesc: The tour consists of chronological stages generated from real data: The Void (pre-GitHub silence), Genesis (ecosystem Big Bang), First Nodes, First Giant (the first major corporation), Acceleration, Epicenter (the most connected org), Stellar Qubit (the most ★ repo), Entanglement (collaboration network), Bridge Users, Cosmic Inflation (boom year), Quantum Babel (language diversity), Consolidation, and the Final Panorama. Each text is unique and dynamically generated from the dataset.
tour.controlsDesc: Use ← → or the forward/back buttons to navigate between waypoints. You can also press Space to advance. The Skip tour ✕ button lets you exit at any time. When finished, you can Restart the entire experience or Exit smoothly to free exploration mode.
tour.timelineDesc: The bottom slider lets you filter the universe by period. Drag the slider control to visualize only entities active up to a specific year. When released, the universe shows or hides nodes based on their activity date. Press ✕ next to the year to restore the full view. During the tour, the slider automatically synchronizes with the narrative.

controls.intro: Navigation and interaction controls for the quantum universe. Every interaction follows the quantum metaphor.
controls.clickDesc: Clicking on an entity "collapses its wave function," revealing all its information: collaboration radar, statistics, network metrics, resilience, DNA, and more. The camera smoothly moves toward the selected entity.
controls.scrollDesc: The mouse wheel controls the zoom level. The Level of Detail (LOD) system automatically adjusts visual complexity based on distance: from far away only dots are visible, up close you see the full details of each entity.
controls.dragDesc: Drag with the mouse to orbit around the universe. The camera follows a spherical orbit centered on the selected point of interest, or at the center of the universe if nothing is selected.
controls.escDesc: Closes the detail panel if an entity is selected. If nothing is selected, closes the full visualization and returns to the main dashboard.
controls.searchDesc: The top bar lets you search for any entity by name. Results are grouped by type (organizations, repositories, users) and bridge users are marked with a golden label. Selecting a result centers the camera on the entity.
controls.settingsDesc: The settings menu allows: toggling zone borders, showing/hiding bots, highlighting specific entity types with color filters, and activating Simple Mode — which disables all ambient effects (cosmic rays, Dyson Shell, gravitational waves, quantum foam, interference grid) leaving only the real data visible, like cooling a quantum processor to millikelvins.
controls.pinDesc: In the detail panel, use the pin button to pin an entity and then select another to compare them side by side. Bars show both entities' metrics overlaid with a delta indicating the difference.
controls.expandDesc: The detail panel can expand to full view with the maximize button, showing a 2-column layout with all metrics, collaboration matrix, knowledge flows, and complete entity analysis.
